key_points: Composite solids: divide into simple solids (cylinder, cone, sphere, prism), calculate SA/volume separately, then sum or subtract as needed.
Hollow or cylindrical shells: Material volume = Outer volume − Inner volume.
Frustum of cone: Use l = √(h² + (R−r)²) for slant height; CSA = π(R+r)l, Volume = (1/3)πh(R² + r² + Rr).
Sphere with cylindrical hole: Volume = (4/3)πR³ − πr²h (cylinder volume).
Conversions between units: Ensure all dimensions in same unit (cm³ → m³ etc.).
formulas: Cone: CSA = πrl; TSA = πr(l+r); Volume = (1/3)πr²h
Hollow cylinder: Volume = πh(R²−r²); CSA = 2πh(R+r) + 2π(R²−r²) for open or closed ends
Composite solids: Total SA = sum of outer surfaces minus common surfaces; Total volume = sum of volumes minus overlapping parts
examples: A cylinder of height 14 cm and radius 7 cm has a conical cavity of height 7 cm and base radius 7 cm. Find CSA of material. Cylinder CSA = 2π7×14 = 196π; Cone CSA = π7×7 = 49π; Total CSA = 196π + 49π = 245π ≈ 769.7 cm²
A solid sphere of radius 10 cm has a cylindrical hole of radius 6 cm through the center. Volume of remaining solid = (4/3)π10³ − π6²×12 ≈ 4188.79 − 1357.17 ≈ 2831.6 cm³
A hemisphere of radius 7 cm is attached to a cylinder of radius 7 cm and height 14 cm. Find TSA. Cylinder CSA = 2π7×14=196π, Hemisphere TSA = 2π7²=98π; Total TSA = 196π + 98π = 294π ≈ 923.63 cm²
exam_tips: Always identify overlapping surfaces in composite solids to avoid double counting.
Use Pythagoras theorem to find slant heights for cones and frustums.
For hollow solids, subtract inner surface area and volume correctly.
Check whether the base is included for TSA calculation (closed/open solids).
Draw diagrams wherever possible to visualize composite solids.
